behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure has two parts — HMAC comparison returning false AND the handler not calling enqueue. Testing only the crypto function (Unit) misses the wiring; we need to verify the handler short-circuits correctly. REAL-INFRA: No OS/subprocess/network required; a mock queue injected into the handler fully exposes the failure. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the signature-check function would vacuously pass, so the real HMAC logic must execute. REJECTION-GATE for System: no external command or real HTTP server is required — an in-process HTTP test client plus mock queue reproduces the exact failure.
behaviors.test_description: Mount the handler with a real HMAC implementation and a spy/mock queue. Send a POST with a valid body but a tampered X-Signature-256 header. Assert the response is 401 and the queue's write method was never called.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is an ordering guarantee across the handler→queue module boundary; it cannot be caught by testing either in isolation. A Unit test of the handler function alone cannot verify that the response is sent after the queue write if both are awaitable. REAL-INFRA: A mock queue that records the timestamp of each call, combined with an in-process HTTP test client that timestamps the response receipt, is sufficient to observe inversion. No real external queue is needed. REJECTION-GATE for System: no subprocess or OS I/O is needed to detect the ordering failure.
behaviors.test_description: Inject a mock queue that records a monotonic timestamp when `write()` is called. Send a valid signed request via an in-process test client and record the timestamp when the 200 response arrives. Assert queue-write timestamp < response-received timestamp.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure (handler awaiting downstream) only manifests when the handler and the downstream processor are wired together. Pure Unit testing of the handler function misses whether the downstream call is accidentally awaited. REAL-INFRA: A mock downstream that blocks for a configurable delay, injected in-process, exposes the failure: if the handler awaits it, the response will be delayed by the mock's sleep. No real HTTP server or subprocess is required. REJECTION-GATE for System: concurrency semantics here follow from whether the handler awaits a promise, not from OS-level scheduling — an in-process mock reproduces the failure faithfully.
behaviors.test_description: Inject a mock downstream processor that sleeps for 500 ms before resolving. Send a valid request via an in-process test client and assert the 200 response arrives well under 500 ms, confirming the handler did not await downstream completion.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: The behavior requires (a) real wall-clock elapsed time, (b) truly concurrent HTTP connections, and (c) earlier slow requests actively consuming processing capacity. If the test harness serializes requests on a shared in-process event loop, it may never expose the blocking path — the concurrency failure mode is hidden. A real HTTP server with multiple simultaneous TCP connections is the bright line: only a real server properly exercises the scheduler under concurrent load. MOCK-HIDE: A mock that serializes calls would always pass. REJECTION-GATE for Integration: we cannot point to a concrete disqualifying reason why an in-process test would reliably reproduce concurrent-connection contention; the 'wall-clock under real concurrency' condition demands a real listening socket.
behaviors.test_description: Start the service as a real local HTTP server. Launch N concurrent client requests, each sending a valid event, while a slow downstream mock holds N-1 of them in processing for 10+ seconds. Assert that each new incoming request receives a 200 response within 3 seconds wall-clock time regardless of the backlogged downstream.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is at the boundary between the handler and the deduplication store — it cannot be caught by testing either module in isolation. The handler must consult the ID-tracking store before calling queue.write(); only the wired combination reveals whether the lookup is missing or the check is bypassed. REAL-INFRA: An in-process ID store (even a real local DB or in-memory set used as the real implementation) reproduces the failure when two requests with the same event ID arrive. No external service or OS-level I/O is required for the deduplication logic itself. REJECTION-GATE for System: the failure mode is application-layer state management, not OS/subprocess behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the handler with the real deduplication store and a spy queue. Send two POST requests carrying identical event IDs (both with valid signatures). Assert the queue's write method is called exactly once, and the second request receives a 200 (or idempotent acknowledgment) without a second queue write.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is at the handler→queue boundary: the handler must read the queue's 'full' signal and translate it into the correct HTTP error response with the required header. Testing the error-mapping function alone (Unit) does not verify that the queue error is captured and propagated correctly. REAL-INFRA: A mock queue configured to throw/return a 'full' error is sufficient; no real queue infrastructure is needed. REJECTION-GATE for Unit: the behavior requires observing the HTTP response (status + header), which requires the handler to be exercised as a whole, not just an isolated error-mapping function.
behaviors.test_description: Inject a mock queue whose write method rejects with a 'queue full' error. Send a valid signed request and assert the response status is 503 and the Retry-After header is present with a sensible value.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: This behavior tests the queue module itself, not the handler. The failure — capacity not enforced — lives inside the queue implementation. A Unit test of an isolated count-check function could miss whether the real backing store's count query is correct or whether concurrent writes bypass the guard. REAL-INFRA: 'Persistent' implies a real backing store (e.g., a local DB, SQLite, or file). The capacity enforcement must be exercised against the actual storage layer; an in-memory mock would trivially pass or trivially fail without reflecting real persistence semantics. A local embedded DB (SQLite or similar in a test environment) suffices — no external service required. REJECTION-GATE for Unit: the 'actually enforces' phrasing specifically calls out that we need the real implementation under real persistence, not a stub.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the queue with a capacity of N (e.g., 3). Write N events through the real queue implementation backed by a local test DB. Assert all N writes succeed. Attempt a write of event N+1 and assert the queue raises a capacity-exceeded error (which the handler should surface as 503).
